title: Unit-Level Estimation
text: Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly rather than starting from domain-level direct estimates. They can be powerful, but they require much stronger data conditions than area-level Fay-Herriot models.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
1 Understanding Unit-Level Estimation
In area-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from one direct estimate per domain. In unit-level Small Area Estimation, the model starts from individual or household survey records:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where \(y_{di}\) is the outcome for unit \(i\) in domain \(d\), \(x_{di}\) is a vector of unit-level covariates, \(u_d\) is the area effect, and \(e_{di}\) is unit-level noise. For binary outcomes, the model is usually written as a logistic or probit mixed model.
The domain estimate is then built by predicting or simulating values for the population units in each domain and aggregating them:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
\]
This makes unit-level SAE a bottom-up approach: model people or households first, predict for the whole target population, and then aggregate to the geographic domain of choice.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
2 Caveats with Unit-Level Estimation
Unit-level SAE is useful when the survey and population data share comparable individual, household, and covariates. It is most appropriate when the outcome is strongly related to person- or household-level characteristics rather than to an area, or when estimates may need to be aggregated to more than one geography.






Pros:

Uses individual or household covariates directly, rather than area averages
Can aggregate to several geographic scales

Cons:

Survey and population datasets need to be harmonised and include the same covariates
More sensitive estimates because predictions are made for many non-sampled units and scaled up to population level
More technically demanding, especially in terms of privacy/access




In general, prefer Area-level SAE when only domain-level covariates are available. Unit-level SAE is more realistic when suitable population microdata, or a validated synthetic population can provide the prediction population.

title: Unit-Level Estimation
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use SUMMER::smoothUnit() to fit a basic unit-level SAE model from a survey::svydesign() object and population unit-level covariates. It supports using an adjacency matrix for spatial structure.

title: Troubleshooting
text: Lorem Ipsum

title: Preparing SAE Inputs
text: A survey outcome becomes SAE-ready through a clear estimand, a coded indicator, a target domain, a domain size, and a compatible survey weight. To see this workflow in action, go to Tutorial: Data Preparation.

title: Preparing SAE Inputs
text:
1 Defining the Estimand
Before fitting any SAE model, define exactly what the survey estimate should represent. This so-called estimand determines how SAE-ready inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream. It is good practice to formulate the estimands of interest in this format:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which geographic domains.

E.g.,

Share of adults who use the bus weekly across MSOA
Mean commute distance among workers across Wards

title: Preparing SAE Inputs
text:
2 Constructing the Indicator
After defining the estimand, identify or create the survey indicator that measures the outcome. Here are some of the most common indicator types:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the bus weeklyIndicator: Uses bus weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean commute distance among workers Indicator: Commute distance in miles



Use the survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.

title: Preparing SAE Inputs
section: 3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights
text:
3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights

3.1 Domain size
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE pipeline. Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, workers, commuters, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.

The choice of target domain, besides being shaped by the estimand definition, should also be informed by data availability. For example,

if survey respondent records are only identified at the Local Authority level and no finer, then Local Authority is the most granular target domain for SAE
if the domain sizes are only available at the Ward level and no finer (e.g., total worker population by Ward), then it is recommended to use Ward as the most granular target domain for SAE

[ADD IMAGE]


3.2 Survey weight
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions. The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. In general, weight variables are derived to adjust for one or more aspects, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For a SAE workflow, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. Moreover, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable, as it implies different estimation approaches.

Some weights are grossing weights \(w^{G}\). They make proportions representative of the target population and sum to a population total or domain size \(N_d\).
Other weights are analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but often sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).

If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \(w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}\) if required.
[ADD IMAGE]






Note



For the SAE workflow, the preferred setup is one where grossing weights are provided for each survey respondent record and domain sizes are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population count in each domain.
If the domain size is unknown and/or no compatible weight variables are provided for the estimand of interest, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be redefined (e.g., coarser geography, less specific target population).

title: Preparing SAE Inputs
text:
4 Preparing SAE inputs
Putting everything together, each of the estimands of interest should have a short record as follows that will guide how we prepare input datasets for the SAE workflow. For example, a compact record could be written as:













#
Estimand
Indicator
Indicator construction
Target domain
Domain size
Weight variable
Other remarks




1
Share of adults who use the bus weekly across MSOAs
is_freq_bus_user
Binary: 1 if BusUseFreq = 1; 0 if adult does not use the bus weekly; null for invalid or refused responses
MSOA
Adult population aged 16+ by MSOA
AdultWeight; applies to all adult respondents; grossing weight
Stratified by age_group


2
Mean commute distance among workers across Wards
commute_distance
Continuous: numeric miles from TravelWorkDistance; only workers with valid commute distance are in scope
Ward
Worker population by Ward
CommuteModuleWeight; applies to commute module respondents (a subsample)
—



Finally, each estimand should have enough information to create:

a survey indicator file with one row per sampled unit and the fields unit_id, domain_id, indicator, and weight;
a domain-size file with one row per target domain and the fields domain_id, domain_size (optionally, domain_name);

[ADD IMAGE SUMMARY OF PROCESS]

title: Direct Estimation
text: Direct estimates are the first estimation step in an area-level SAE workflow. They show what the survey can say before borrowing strength from covariates or spatial structure.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Inputs
The direct-estimation step uses the standard files prepared earlier:







File
Required fields




Survey indicator file
unit_id, domain_id, indicator, weight


Domain-size file
domain_id, domain_size



The indicator column can be binary, such as 1 for adults who walk weekly and 0 otherwise, or numeric for a continuous outcome.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Direct Estimator and Sampling Variances
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.








Situation
Estimator
When to use




Domain size is known and grossing weight is available
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
Anchors estimates to true domain size and gives design-unbiased levels; required for estimating counts or totals


Domain size is unknown and/or only analysis weights are available
Hájek estimator
Preferred for means or proportions when using normalised analysis weights and no reliable domain size is available



The Horvitz-Thompson estimator calculates the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) in domain (\(d\)) as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR} =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}{N_d}
\]
The Hájek Estimator calculates the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d\) in domain (\(d\)) as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR} =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}
{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}}.
\]
where \(N_d\) is domain_size, \(w_{di}\) is weight, and \(y_{di}\) is indicator.
Direct estimates are paired with estimated sampling variances, telling us how much uncertainty comes from observing only a sample within each domain. In practice, the variance depends on the estimator, weight scale, and survey design information available.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Next Step
Use the direct estimates and sampling variances to decide whether direct reporting is enough, or whether model-based SAE is needed.



Scenario
Next step




Most domains have usable sample sizes and acceptable uncertainty
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Many domains have no sample, or sampling variances are missing, zero, or unreasonable
Revisit the domain choice, estimand, or input data before modelling.


Direct estimates are imprecise and a quick model-based approach is enough
Fit a Frequentist Area-Level SAE model to borrow strength from covariates.


Direct estimates are imprecise, spatial effects matter, or uncertainty needs to be reported as probabilities
Fit a Bayesian Area-Level SAE with Spatial model to borrow strength from covariates and neighbouring areas.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
4 Code Implementation






Tip



Use sae::direct() to calculate direct estimates by domain. It returns core direct-estimation outputs including domain sample size (SampSize), direct estimate (Direct), estimated standard deviation (SD), and coefficient of variation (CV). Square SD to obtain the Variance.
In sae::direct(), if domsize is provided, the Horvitz-Thompson direct estimator is used; if not provided, the Hajek ratio direct estimator is used.
If the survey includes more detailed design information, use the survey package for the direct-estimation step. This gives design-aware domain estimates and uncertainty summaries.



4.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian) + Spatial
text: This notebook extends the area-level Fay-Herriot workflow. It first reframes area-level SAE in Bayesian terms, then shows why spatial structure may be useful when neighbouring domains share unexplained patterns.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian) + Spatial
text:
1 Bayesian versus Frequentist approach
Both frequentist and Bayesian area-level SAE start from the same basic Fay-Herriot idea that the domain direct estimates are :
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
The difference is how the unknown quantities are estimated and how uncertainty is interpreted.

In the frequentist approach, unknown quantities such as \(\beta\), \(\sigma_u^2\), and \(u_d\) are estimated as fixed unknowns. Typical outputs are one point estimate per area (EBLUP), uncertainty measures such as RMSE, and confidence intervals.
In the Bayesian approach, these unknown quantities are instead treated as uncertain and assigned prior distributions. Typical outputs are posterior means, credible intervals, and probability summaries.

This means that, with a Bayesian approach, each area has a distribution of plausible values. This makes it natural to report a central estimate, an uncertainty interval, and probability-style summaries, such as how likely an area is to be above a certain policy threshold.
[ADD IMAGE]
Therefore, in practice, there is little need to perform Area-Level SAE using both approaches sequentially, especially when there are suitable R packages that have simplified the more complex implementation of the Bayesian approach.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian) + Spatial
text:
2 Spatial Structure
Going back to the FH model equation cited above, the non-spatial area-level model assumes the unexplained area effects \(u_d\) are independent after accounting for covariates. In urban research, this is rarely true. This extension of the Fay-Herriot model assumes spatial correlation between areas, i.e., the values of one area will be influenced by the values of the areas around it.
\[
u = \rho W u + \eta,\qquad \eta \sim N(0,\sigma^2 I)
\]
Adding spatial structure, usually a binary spatial weights matrix \(W\), lets the model borrow strength not only from covariates, but also from nearby areas.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Area-Level Estimation (Bayesian) + Spatial
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



For Bayesian area-level SAE, the main package used here is SUMMER. It fits area-level smoothing models using INLA, and can work either from direct estimates or from survey microdata, greatly simplifying the implementation of the Bayesian approach and adding spatial structure in Area-Level SAE.

Use SUMMER::smoothArea() when you already have direct estimates and their variances.
Use SUMMER::smoothSurvey() when you want SUMMER to calculate design-adjusted direct estimates from survey microdata before smoothing.

The sae package, which applies a Frequentist approach to SAE, does not handle out-of-sample domains (i.e., domains without respondent records) and spatial structure automatically. These cases require extra implementation work.



3.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
Beginner-friendly Small Area Estimation (SAE) for researchers estimating survey-derived metrics across a whole country or region at fine geographic resolution.
The site has two tracks: a theory track that explains the SAE workflow, and a practical tutorial track that applies the workflow to a dataset. It uses the National Survey for Wales as the first worked survey.
The main practical uses R, Quarto, and SAE packages such as survey, sae, and SUMMER. Make sure you have latest R version installed.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
1 Why Small Area Estimation?
[draft] Survey are often not precise enough for fine geographies such as Local Authorities, MSOAs, or LSOAs. Some areas have very few/no respondents, unequal weights Taking weighted meands can be unstable. Also out of domain sample
SAE is useful when direct estimates are too noisy or incomplete. Area-level SAE use direct estimates and their sampling variances, borrow strength across areas using covariates and random effects.
SAE can be further improved with bayesian statistics (to smooth out area) and add spatial structure (spatial autocorrelation)
SAE combines survey data, population estimates, auxiliary data, modelling to estimate the unsurveyed in an informed way.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
2 Methods
Use these pages to understand the concepts and decisions behind the workflow.



No.
Step
Objective




1
Preparing the Inputs
Define what is being estimated, for whom, and at what geographical scale.


2
Direct estimation
Estimate area-level indicators directly from the survey and assess their precision.


3a
Area-Level SAE (Frequentist)
Fit a Fay-Herriot model that improves unstable direct estimates by borrowing strength from covariates (Frequentist approach).


3b
Area-Level SAE (Bayesian) with Spatial
Reframe the area-level model with posterior uncertainty, then add spatial structure to borrow strength from neighbouring areas.


4
Unit-Level SAE
A bottom-up approach to SAE using individual person/household records instead of area-level estimates.


5
Diagnose and Report
Check model performance, report uncertainty, and present estimates.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
3 Resources
Supporting materials for adapting the workflow:

SAE Toolkit collects reusable input formats and helper scripts.
Troubleshooting lists common data, estimation, and reporting problems.

title: Diagnostics, Reporting, and Benchmarking
text: SAE methods is best visualised as a pipeline and their outputs should be evaluated at each step to decide whether estimates and uncertainties are good enough to report.

title: Diagnostics, Reporting, and Benchmarking
text:
1 Evaluate the Estimates and Benchmarking
Focus on a few checks:

Direct vs modelled: do modelled estimates stay close to precise direct estimates?
Uncertainty: which domains still have large SE, RMSE, RSE, or wide credible intervals?
Model effect: which domains move most after adding covariates or spatial structure?
Spatial pattern: do residuals still show geographic structure?

Finally, compare aggregated final estimates with known higher-level totals, if available. This helps check whether the small-area estimates remain consistent with trusted regional or national figures. This step is more important as the estimation workflow becomes more complex, especially for unit-level estimation approach.

title: Diagnostics, Reporting, and Benchmarking
text:
2 Report Estimates and Uncertainty
Report the uncertainty measure that matches the method used:







Workflow
Uncertainty to report




Direct estimation
standard error, confidence interval, RSE


Frequentist Fay-Herriot
MSE, RMSE, approximate confidence interval


Bayesian or spatial SAE
credible interval, posterior probability, threshold probability


Unit-level SAE
bootstrap MSE/RMSE, simulation interval, credible interval, or posterior interval depending on the model









Note



Useful outputs include estimate maps, uncertainty maps, direct-versus-model plots, and flags for domains where estimates are mostly model-driven.

title: Diagnostics, Reporting, and Benchmarking
text:
3 Code Implementation

3.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text: Frequentist area-level SAE is the next step when direct estimates are too noisy or unstable, or when there are domains with weak survey information. The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model, which combines direct estimates with domain-level covariates to improve precision.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
1 Understanding the Fay-Herriot model
Fay-Herriot combines two pieces of information for each domain:

the direct estimate from the survey, and
a covariate-based model prediction.

The direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d^{DIR}\) can be written as the covariate-explained part \(x_d^\top\beta\), plus an area-specific unexplained effect \(u_d\), plus survey sampling noise \(e_d\).
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]
The FH estimate, is a weighted average of the direct estimate and the covariate-based prediction:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\hat{\gamma}_d \hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\hat{\gamma}_d) x_d^\top\hat{\beta}.
\]
The shrinkage weight \(\hat{\gamma}_d = \frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}{\hat{\sigma}_u^2 + \psi_d}\), where \(\psi_d\) is the sampling variance of the direct estimate, controls how much the FH estimate “trusts” the direct estimate:

if \(\psi_d\) is small, the direct estimate is precise, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 1;
if \(\psi_d\) is large, the direct estimate is noisy, so \(\hat{\gamma}_d\) is closer to 0.

In practice, FH leaves precise direct estimates mostly unchanged, while pulling noisy direct estimates toward the covariate-based prediction.






Note



In this frequentist FH workflow, the main outputs are the model-based estimate (EBLUP) and its uncertainty (RMSE).

If EBLUPs stay close to precise direct estimates and RMSE is acceptable, the estimates may be suitable for reporting.
If results are sensitive to covariate choice or diagnostics show poor fit, revisit the covariates and refit the model.

title: Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
2 Covariate Choices
Domain-level covariates can often be found via census data and other population-level aggregate statistics. Choose covariates with emphasis on coverage and comparability across domains.

Coverage: each covariate should be available for all target domains.
Alignment: match geography, definition, and reference period to the survey indicator.
Relevance: keep variables with a clear substantive link to the outcome and avoid including many weak predictors.
Predictive: choose covariates that improve domain-level prediction and are easy to explain; avoid downstream variables, and remove or combine highly correlated predictors.

Common examples of covariates when estimating transport-related indicators are: age, income, employment profile, population density, housing mix, deprivation, urban/rural classification, transport accessibility metrics, car ownership, etc.
[ADD IMAGE]

title: Area-Level Estimation (Frequentist)
text:
3 Code Implementation






Tip



Use sae::eblupFH() to fit a frequentist Fay-Herriot model and sae::mseFH() to estimate uncertainty.
sae does not handle out-of-sample domains (i.e., domains without respondent records) automatically. These domains require separate model-based predictions from the fitted covariate relationship.



3.1 Worked Example
Lorem Ipsum - share real example National Survey of Wales

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is meant for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
A SAE workflow usually needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per target domain
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or derive spatial structure, if relevant
(optional) population unit file when fitting unit-level SAE


1.1 Survey indicator file:
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, target domain, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
If available, a domain-size file gives the denominator for each target domain. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for creating adjacency matrix for spatial models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the reporting domain.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every target domain. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every target domain.





1.5 Files for unit-level estimation
Unit-level SAE needs two harmonised files:

The survey_unit_data file besides indicator also needs covariates used to fit the model








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Outcome to model.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.


covariate_*
Individual or household covariate.




The population_unit_data file provides the prediction population (real or synthetic microdata), with one row per population unit, household, or population cell. The covariates must match those in survey_unit_data.








Column
Meaning




unit_id
Population person, household, or synthetic unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code used to aggregate predictions.


covariate_*
Matching individual or household covariate also used in survey_unit_data.


pop_weight
Population count represented by the row (optional, in case population cells are used)





1.6 Recommended output schema
For diagnostics, each estimation script should end with a domain-level results table using the same column names. This makes it easier to combine direct, area-level, spatial, and unit-level outputs.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


method
Estimation method label.


estimate
Point estimate for the domain.


se
Standard error or posterior standard deviation, where available.


lower
Lower confidence or credible interval bound.


upper
Upper confidence or credible interval bound.


uncertainty_type
Type of interval, such as confidence_interval or credible_interval.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by domain, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


area_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level model with covariates and spatial structure derived from boundary geometry, if relevant.


unit_level_sae.R
Fit Bayesian unit-level model using survey microdata and population or synthetic-population covariates.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.

title: Worked Example
text: This page applies the SAE input preparation flow to the National Survey for Wales 2022-23. The main purpose is to show the decisions needed to turn survey data into SAE-ready inputs.

title: Worked Example
text:
1 Start with the Estimands
Each input file starts from an estimand: the outcome, target population, and geography the estimate should represent. The target population matters because it determines which survey weight and domain size can be used. Since Local Authority is the finest spatial identifier provided, it is used as the target domain.
These transport-related estimands of interest are defined as follows:










Estimand
Indicator
Target population
Target domain
Remarks




Mean commute distance among workers
commute_distance
Workers aged 16+
Local Authority
Routed question for commuters only


Share of adults dissatisfied with bus services
poor_bus_satisfaction
Adults aged 16+
Local Authority
Routed question for bus users only


Share of adults who walk as main transport at least weekly
walk_weekly
Adults aged 16+
Local Authority
Active-travel module subsample

title: Worked Example
text:
2 Translate the Estimands into Inputs
The table below shows how the indicators should be prepared, and links each to the weight, and domain-size decision.










Indicator
Source variables
Coding rule
Weight
Domain size




commute_distance
TravWkDist
numeric commute distance in miles, non-commuters are coded NA
SampleAdultWeight, analysis weight
Worker population


poor_bus_satisfaction
BusOverSat
binary; 1 if fairly or very dissatisfied, non-users are coded NA
WalesAdultWeight grossing weight
Adult population by Local Authority


walk_weekly
AtFrqWlk10
binary; 1 if walking for transport at least weekly
WalesTravelWeight, derived from SampleTravelWeight
Adult population by Local Authority



For SAE workflows to produce estimates at the population level, a grossing weight is generally preferred when a matching domain size is available. In this dataset, both WalesAdultWeight and SampleAdultWeight are provided, but they are used differently:

WalesAdultWeight is used when the adult domain size is part of the estimator, while
SampleAdultWeight is used when no compatible worker domain size is available.

For the active-travel estimand, SampleTravelWeight is scaled to a grossing weight because adult domain sizes are available.

title: Worked Example
text:
3 Data Processing

3.1 Load data

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

project_root <- if (dir.exists("pages")) "." else ".."

raw_dir <- file.path(project_root, "data", "raw")
census_root <- file.path(raw_dir, "census")
clean_dir <- file.path(project_root, "data", "clean")
dir.create(clean_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

nsw_raw <- read_tsv(
  file.path(raw_dir, "nsw_2022-23_respondent_file_for_ukds.tab"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  rename(unit_id = CaseNo)     # rename for consistency

Gather and clean LA codes from the survey to be used as domain_id field.

survey_domains <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    domain_id = str_c("W0", as.integer(DvLA) + 6000000L),   # Build domain_id
    domain_id = recode(         # Fix code for 2 LAs, differ in two sources for technical reasons
      domain_id,
      "W06000007" = "W06000023", # Powys
      "W06000017" = "W06000024"  # Merthyr Tydfil
    )
  )



3.2 Aggregating Domain Size
In this exercise, assume we only have UK population estimates (not worker estimates). We therefore construct the required domain size (the adult population by Local Authority) from the population estimates.

adult_domain_sizes <- read_csv(
  file.path(census_root, "census2021-ts007-ltla.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size =
      `Age: Total; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 4 years and under; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 5 to 9 years; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 10 to 15 years; measures: Value`
  )
  
adult_domain_sizes

# A tibble: 22 × 3
   domain_id domain_name      domain_size
   <chr>     <chr>                  <dbl>
 1 W06000001 Isle of Anglesey       57146
 2 W06000002 Gwynedd                97974
 3 W06000003 Conwy                  96364
 4 W06000004 Denbighshire           78907
 5 W06000005 Flintshire            127581
 6 W06000006 Wrexham               110270
 7 W06000008 Ceredigion             61387
 8 W06000009 Pembrokeshire         102555
 9 W06000010 Carmarthenshire       155484
10 W06000011 Swansea               197864
# ℹ 12 more rows




3.3 Building Indicators
Define the missing-value rules used when constructing survey indicators.

missing_codes <- c(-99, -98, -9, -8, -7)

is_missing_code <- function(x) {
  is.na(x) | x %in% missing_codes
}

make_binary <- function(x, yes_values) {
  case_when(
    is_missing_code(x) ~ NA_integer_,
    x %in% yes_values ~ 1L,
    TRUE ~ 0L
  )
}

Calculate the indicators based on the rules set out above.

# commute_distance
commute_distance_indicator <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    commute_distance = case_when(
      is_missing_code(TravWkDist) ~ NA_real_,
      TRUE ~ as.numeric(TravWkDist)
    )
  )

# poor_bus_satisfaction
bus_satisfaction_indicator <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    poor_bus_satisfaction = make_binary(BusOverSat, yes_values = c(4, 5))
  )

# walk_weekly
walk_indicator <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    walk_weekly = make_binary(AtFrqWlk10, yes_values = c(1, 2, 3))
  )

survey_indicators <- commute_distance_indicator |>
  left_join(bus_satisfaction_indicator, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(walk_indicator, by = "unit_id")

survey_indicators |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
    unit_id commute_distance poor_bus_satisfaction walk_weekly
      <dbl>            <dbl>                 <int>       <int>
1 222300001               NA                    NA          NA
2 222300002               NA                    NA          NA
3 222300003               NA                    NA          NA
4 222300004               NA                    NA          NA
5 222300005               NA                     1          NA




3.4 Weight
Filter for the relevant weight variables from the survey.

survey_weights <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id,
    SampleAdultWeight,
    WalesAdultWeight,
    SampleTravelWeight
  )

survey_weights |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
    unit_id SampleAdultWeight WalesAdultWeight SampleTravelWeight
      <dbl>             <dbl>            <dbl>              <dbl>
1 222300001             0.647            148.                  NA
2 222300002             0.344             78.8                 NA
3 222300003             1.34             306.                  NA
4 222300004             1.31             299.                  NA
5 222300005             0.676            155.                  NA


SampleTravelWeight is an analysis weight for the active-travel module. Because walk_weekly will use the adult domain size later, create a grossing-style WalesTravelWeight by scaling SampleTravelWeight within each Local Authority so that the weights sum to the adult population in each Local Authority.

# First, calculate the current total `SampleTravelWeight` in each domain.

survey_weights_with_domain <- survey_weights |>
  left_join(survey_domains, by = "unit_id")

travel_weight_totals <- survey_weights_with_domain |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  summarise(
    sample_travel_weight_total = sum(SampleTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

travel_weight_totals |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  domain_id sample_travel_weight_total
  <chr>                          <dbl>
1 W06000001                       45.7
2 W06000002                       80.1
3 W06000003                       76.7
4 W06000004                       61.9
5 W06000005                      102. 

# Then attach the adult domain size and calculate the scaled travel weight.
survey_weights_scaled <- survey_weights_with_domain |>
  left_join(
    adult_domain_sizes |>
      select(domain_id, domain_size),
    by = "domain_id"
  ) |>
  left_join(travel_weight_totals, by = "domain_id") |>
  mutate(
    WalesTravelWeight = case_when(
      !is.na(SampleTravelWeight) & sample_travel_weight_total > 0 ~
        SampleTravelWeight * domain_size / sample_travel_weight_total,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  )

survey_weights_scaled |>
  filter(!is.na(WalesTravelWeight)) |>
  select(unit_id, domain_id, SampleTravelWeight, WalesTravelWeight) |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 4
    unit_id domain_id SampleTravelWeight WalesTravelWeight
      <dbl> <chr>                  <dbl>             <dbl>
1 222300006 W06000001              0.550              688.
2 222300007 W06000001              0.405              507.
3 222300008 W06000001              0.869             1087.
4 222300011 W06000001              0.635              794.
5 222300017 W06000001              0.275              344.

# Keep only the weight columns needed later.

survey_weights <- survey_weights_scaled |>
  select(
    unit_id,
    SampleAdultWeight,
    WalesAdultWeight,
    SampleTravelWeight,
    WalesTravelWeight
  )

survey_weights |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 5 × 5
    unit_id SampleAdultWeight WalesAdultWeight SampleTravelWeight
      <dbl>             <dbl>            <dbl>              <dbl>
1 222300001             0.647            148.                  NA
2 222300002             0.344             78.8                 NA
3 222300003             1.34             306.                  NA
4 222300004             1.31             299.                  NA
5 222300005             0.676            155.                  NA
# ℹ 1 more variable: WalesTravelWeight <dbl>




3.5 Check Survey Inputs
At this point, we will check non-NA values of each indicator to evaluate ‘missingness’ and whether it is expected

survey_inputs <- survey_domains |>
  left_join(survey_indicators, by = "unit_id") |>
  left_join(survey_weights, by = "unit_id")

survey_inputs |>
  summarise(across(
    c(commute_distance, poor_bus_satisfaction, walk_weekly),
    \(x) sum(!is.na(x))
  )) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = "indicator",
    values_to = "non_missing"
  )

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  indicator             non_missing
  <chr>                       <int>
1 commute_distance             3628
2 poor_bus_satisfaction        8980
3 walk_weekly                  1999


The number of non-missing observations reflects both survey routing and module design: - walk_weekly comes from a subsampled active-travel module of about 2,000 respondents. Its non-missing count is therefore expected; the indicator can still support population-level estimation when paired with the scaled WalesTravelWeight - commute_distance has relatively few non-missing values because it is only asked of respondents who travel to work. This supports the decision to use an analysis-weight approach to estimate proportions (i.e. ‘share of workers’). - poor_bus_satisfaction has a reasonably large number of non-missing responses, despite being routed to only bus users. It can be estimated at the population level with caution.

title: Worked Example
text:
4 Outputs
Keep a small record of the decisions behind each estimand.

estimand_record <- tibble::tribble(
  ~indicator, ~estimand, ~target_population, ~weight, ~domain_size_file, ~preparation_note,
  "commute_distance",
  "Mean commute distance among workers",
  "Workers aged 16+",
  "SampleAdultWeight",
  NA_character_,
  "Uses adult analysis weight; no compatible worker domain size is prepared.",
  "poor_bus_satisfaction",
  "Share of adults dissatisfied with bus services",
  "Adults aged 16+",
  "WalesAdultWeight",
  "adult_domain_sizes.csv",
  "Uses adult weight and adult domain size.",
  "walk_weekly",
  "Share of adults who walk for transport at least weekly",
  "Adults aged 16+",
  "WalesTravelWeight",
  "adult_domain_sizes.csv",
  "Uses active-travel module weight scaled to adult domain size."
)

estimand_record |>
  select(indicator, target_population, weight, domain_size_file) |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  indicator             target_population weight            domain_size_file    
  <chr>                 <chr>             <chr>             <chr>               
1 commute_distance      Workers aged 16+  SampleAdultWeight <NA>                
2 poor_bus_satisfaction Adults aged 16+   WalesAdultWeight  adult_domain_sizes.…
3 walk_weekly           Adults aged 16+   WalesTravelWeight adult_domain_sizes.…


Write a survey input file for each estimand and its associated weight variable. Also export the domain sizes and estimand record so the estimand definition, weight choice, and domain-size decision remain documented.

# create survey input for each estimand
for (i in seq_len(nrow(estimand_record))) {
  spec <- estimand_record[i, ]

  indicator_file <- survey_inputs |>
    transmute(
      unit_id,
      domain_id,
      indicator = .data[[spec$indicator]],
      weight = .data[[spec$weight]]
    )

  write_csv(
    indicator_file,
    file.path(clean_dir, str_c("ind_weight_", spec$indicator, ".csv"))
  )
}

write_csv(estimand_record, file.path(clean_dir, "estimand_record.csv"))
write_csv(adult_domain_sizes, file.path(clean_dir, "adult_domain_sizes.csv"))

We have produced:

data/clean/adult_domain_sizes.csv, for adult-population by LA (from Census 2021);
data/clean/ind_weight_commute_distance.csv, with analysis adult weight;
data/clean/ind_weight_poor_bus_satisfaction.csv, with grossing adult weight;
data/clean/ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv, with active-travel module weight scaled to adult domain size;
data/clean/estimand_record.csv, documenting the target population, weight, domain-size decision, and preparation note for each estimand.

The common output schema for the survey input files is:



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent identifier.


domain_id
Reporting domain (LA) code.


indicator
Binary or continuous outcome for the estimand.


weight
Survey weight selected for that estimand.
